### 1. AJP_2017_San-Juan-Mayor-Speaks-Out-Hurricane-Maria_Social-Video_1m16s

**Platform:** AJ+ · **Year:** September 2017 · **Format:** Field interview — crisis journalism
**Runtime:** 1:16 · **Resolution:** 1080p

**Full Transcript:**
> "I'm scared. I'm scared for the people that I'm responsible for, and I'm scared that when we come out of here, the devastation and the loss of lives would be great. So, I'm just scared for my people."
>
> *[On Puerto Rico's electrical grid]:* "The electrical infrastructure in Puerto Rico was very weakened before Hurricane Irma came. So, we're looking at about four months of no electricity."
>
> *[Q: Have you received any word from U.S. officials?]:* "In this state of emergency, I have to say that at least the communication channel has been opened, and we have felt like at least somebody's listening on the other side of the ocean. Which is new. Which is new."
>
> *[Q: What's your advice to people directly in the storm?]:* "First of all, save yourself. Don't think that this is going to get better. Secondly, pray to whomever you want. And number three is Friday, Saturday when this is all over — come out of your house and help some."

**Subject:** San Juan Mayor Carmen Yulín Cruz · **Cultural moment:** Hurricane Maria, September 2017 — Category 4 hurricane devastates Puerto Rico, Trump administration's slow response triggers national debate

**Shot list:**
| Time | Shot Type | Description | Audio |
|------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 0:00 | Medium close-up | Mayor in what appears to be emergency operations center or field location | Direct-to-camera statement |
| 0:15 | Cut | Close-up, Mayor visibly emotional | Voice breaks on "I'm scared" |
| 0:30 | B-roll or continue | Infrastructure framing implied | Mayor discusses electrical grid |
| 0:55 | Medium | Two-shot implied (Q&A format) | Reporter off-camera |
| 1:05 | Close-up | Mayor, composed, direct | Final advice sequence |

**Audio analysis:** No background music — raw interview audio, ambient sound of emergency environment. Treatment is pure verité. Tone: urgent, crisis, human.

**Production role signals:**
- ✅ Field producing (secured access to sitting mayor during active disaster)
- ✅ Interview production (controlled Q&A in chaotic environment)
- ✅ Social optimization (76-second cut — pure social platform length)
- ✅ Talent direction (kept interview focused during crisis emotional state)

**Skills demonstrated:**
- Produced field interview with San Juan Mayor Carmen Yulín Cruz during active Hurricane Maria disaster response, capturing primary source testimony on federal government response gap and 4-month infrastructure projection

**CV-ready bullets:**
- Produced crisis field interview with San Juan Mayor Carmen Yulín Cruz during active Hurricane Maria response at AJ+; testimony became part of AJ+'s Hurricane Maria coverage at peak of national debate over federal disaster response (September 2017)

**AI-native portfolio caption:** Emergency field interview with Puerto Rico mayor during Hurricane Maria — raw crisis journalism at 1:16.

**Tags:** #CrisisJournalism #FieldProducing #HurricaneMaria #PuertoRico #AJP #SocialVideo #CivicJournalism #DisasterCoverage

### 2. AJP_2017_Trumps-War-On-Student-Debt_Social-Video_1m45s

**Platform:** AJ+ · **Year:** 2017 · **Format:** Data-driven explainer video
**Runtime:** 1:45 · **Resolution:** 1080p

**Full Transcript:**
> "Bad news for everyone with student loans. It's about to get a lot harder to pay off that debt. The Treasury Department has announced a plan to raise student loan interest rates. That hike could amount to an almost 20% increase in charges for undergrads. And for those of you who've defaulted on your loans, it gets worse. The federal government plans to sue more people to collect on the country's $138 billion of unpaid student debt.
>
> Normally the government sends debt collectors after you. They're the companies that call you from random numbers at all times during the day. Their aim is to either scare you into paying or to negotiate in hopes of receiving any amount of money. But over the last two years, the government has become more aggressive, taking people to court. The Department of Justice reports that more than 3,300 student loan borrowers have been sued since defaulting on their loans. And that program is expected to grow under President Trump.
>
> Even though the lawsuits began under Obama, the interest hike and Education Secretary Betsy DeVos' latest moves signal an escalation in how the White House handles student debt. DeVos has done away with Obama's guidelines that rewarded companies for giving borrowers accurate information about their payment options. She also ended Obama's ban on lenders who charged fees for past due loans if people started repaying their debt within 60 days of defaulting.
>
> And it's not gonna stop there. DeVos and her husband have financial ties to a debt collection agency that does business directly with the Department of Education. That's a huge conflict of interest now that DeVos can influence and oversee debt collection contracts.
>
> We already know the average college student who borrows money leaves school with about $34,000 in loans. But under this new administration, it looks like that price tag is only going to rise."

**Subject:** Trump administration student loan policy · **Cultural moment:** Betsy DeVos confirmation and early Education Department moves, 2017; Fight for $15/student debt national conversation

**Production role signals:**
- ✅ Scripting (dense, data-rich narration with structured argument)
- ✅ Research/investigative (conflict of interest angle — DeVos husband's debt collection ties)
- ✅ Social optimization (105-second format, hooks at 0:00 and 0:15)

**Key data in piece:** $138B unpaid student debt · 3,300 borrowers sued · 20% interest rate hike · $34K average student debt

**CV-ready bullets:**
- Produced data-driven investigative social video at AJ+ exposing Betsy DeVos conflict of interest (husband's debt collection ties to Dept of Education) amid 20% student loan interest hike announcement; piece contributed to AJ+'s Trump education policy coverage (2017)

**AI-native portfolio caption:** Investigative social video on student debt policy — DeVos conflict of interest uncovered in 1:45 format.

**Tags:** #InvestigativeJournalism #PolicyAnalysis #DataJournalism #TrumpAdministration #AJP #ExplainerVideo #SocialFirst

### 3. AJP_2016-2018_Americas-Longest-War-Afghanistan_Social-Video_1m46s

**Platform:** AJ+ · **Year:** 2017 (Trump troop surge context) · **Format:** Policy explainer
**Runtime:** 1:46 · **Resolution:** 1080p

**Full Transcript:**
> "More American lives will be lost in Afghanistan if troops are once again deployed. And it won't fix anything. The U.S. has had more than 15 years to make a difference in Afghanistan. That includes billions of dollars spent and 100,000 troops deployed.
>
> But despite saying the U.S. should get out of Afghanistan in 2013, President Trump is now ramping up the war. We're not winning in Afghanistan right now. [LOWER THIRD: 'The surge was greenlit after those comments.'] And it follows a recent truck bombing in the Afghan capital of Kabul. 150 people were killed in the blast, marking the deadliest attack the country has seen since the Taliban was removed from power.
>
> The Taliban was ousted by U.S.-led forces back in 2001. In 2013, they made a comeback by taking advantage of infighting between local militias. Today, the Afghan government only has control over 60% of the country. And Trump appears to have no strategy in place to deal with the underlying reasons for the Taliban's resurgence.
>
> Adding more troops won't change that. The Taliban has gained ground by capitalizing on distrust of the Afghan local police. Human rights organizations have accused authorities of raping, murdering and abducting citizens. These crimes have gone uninvestigated because local police have the backing of the U.S. And there's no independent body to provide oversight.
>
> The Taliban has also profited from corruption within the ranks of Afghan security forces. It's a problem that's so widespread, the group often buys U.S. weapons and supplies directly from security officials.
>
> With the help of all these factors, the Taliban has been able to advance at a time when Afghan civilians are losing confidence in their country. What America has done in Afghanistan just isn't working. So what will it take to finally end America's longest war?"

**Subject:** US-Afghanistan War · **Cultural moment:** Trump's 2017 Afghanistan surge announcement; 15+ years of US involvement post-9/11

**Key data in piece:** 100,000 troops deployed · 15+ years of involvement · 150 killed in Kabul truck bombing · Taliban controls 40% of country (Afghan govt 60%)

**Production role signals:**
- ✅ Scripting (sustained, structured policy argument — complex geopolitics in 106 seconds)
- ✅ Research (human rights org sourcing, corruption documentation)
- ✅ Social optimization (editorial perspective headline, Q-hook close)

**CV-ready bullets:**
- Produced foreign policy explainer at AJ+ analyzing Taliban resurgence and structural failures behind US Afghanistan strategy; sustained complex international affairs argument in 106-second social format with primary source citations (2017)

**AI-native portfolio caption:** US-Afghanistan policy explainer — 15 years of structural failure distilled to 1:46 social format.

**Tags:** #ForeignPolicy #InternationalAffairs #AJP #PolicyAnalysis #Afghanistan #DataJournalism #SocialFirst

### 4. AJP_2017_Trumps-War-On-Marijuana_Social-Video_1m55s

**Platform:** AJ+ · **Year:** 2017 · **Format:** Economic argument / policy explainer
**Runtime:** 1:55 · **Resolution:** 1080p

**Full Transcript:**
> "President Trump's press secretary says they want to wage a war on marijuana. This makes absolutely no sense. Trump prides himself on being a strong businessman... So doesn't he know how much money this country could make if we legalize pot?
>
> Let's break it down. In Oregon, the state government made more than $25 million in marijuana tax revenue during the first six months of 2016. In Washington, the state pulled in more than $460 million for the entire year. In Colorado, officials have made more than $720 million from the sale of legal weed in 2016 alone. And this industry creates jobs. In 2015, the demand for marijuana in Colorado created more than 18,000 new jobs, from retailers and growers to even lawyers and small business owners.
>
> Marijuana legalization is an issue that most Americans support. And the weed business could bring back jobs to states like Mississippi that suffer from high rates of poverty and household incomes well below the national average.
>
> The future looks lucrative if Trump chooses to embrace what's already growing here in the United States. California said yes to recreational pot last year. Now the state stands to make $1.6 billion in its first year of legalization. And by 2020, California could create a $6 billion market for the industry.
>
> Maybe Trump will realize that attacking the pot industry goes against his campaign promises of creating jobs or getting the country back on track financially. But according to Sean Spicer, that doesn't seem likely. [SPICER QUOTE]: 'When you see something like the opioid addiction crisis blossoming in so many states around this country, the last thing that we should be doing is encouraging people.'
>
> So what's it going to take for Donald Trump to wake up and smell the green?"

**Subject:** Federal marijuana policy under Trump · **Cultural moment:** Sean Spicer's 2017 marijuana enforcement statement; state legalization momentum (CO, WA, OR, CA Prop 64 passage)

**Key data in piece:** Oregon: $25M in 6 months · Washington: $460M/year · Colorado: $720M in 2016 · 18,000 jobs created in Colorado 2015 · California: projected $1.6B year 1, $6B market by 2020

**Production role signals:**
- ✅ Scripting (economic argument using state-level data — contradicts Trump's own business framing)
- ✅ Research (state revenue figures, job creation numbers, California projections)
- ✅ Narrative structure (contradiction → data → implication → closer)

**CV-ready bullets:**
- Produced economic policy explainer at AJ+ framing marijuana legalization as jobs/revenue issue; assembled multistate revenue data ($720M CO, $460M WA) to reframe political debate in audience-accessible 115-second social format (2017)

**AI-native portfolio caption:** Economic case for marijuana legalization — $720M in Colorado tax revenue, distilled to 1:55 social video.

**Tags:** #EconomicPolicy #DataJournalism #MarijuanaPolicy #AJP #SocialFirst #PolicyExplainer #TrumpAdministration

### 5. AJP_2016-2018_Sincerely-A-Minimum-Wage-Worker_Social-Video_1m57s

**Platform:** AJ+ · **Year:** 2016–2018 · **Format:** First-person documentary / advocacy
**Runtime:** 1:57 · **Resolution:** 1080p

**Full Transcript:**
> "Well, if my daughter were to see this, I would hope that she's proud of me, that she can see that her mother did care about something and that it's worth fighting for.
>
> To my future president, my name's Andrea Meadors, but most people call me Andy. I'm a proud single mother of a beautiful two-year-old. Her name is Catalina. My first job is being a single mom... When I'm not busy raising my daughter, I'm working at McDonald's at 106 and Ewing. I like it a lot because of the appreciation I'm showing for my customers.
>
> I get up early for work every day, and I stretch my paycheck to make ends meet. I only make $10.50 an hour. That's Chicago's current minimum wage. I worry about paying for groceries, clothes, and my daughter's medical bills. She was born prematurely. Because I can't afford most things, sometimes I have to accept hand-me-down clothes for Catalina. I also have to cancel doctor appointments and sometimes take leftover food from family and friends.
>
> Recently, I qualified for food stamps, which is a slight improvement. But I shouldn't have to rely on government assistance to get by. The kind of life I live takes a toll on my daughter and me. It's hard, but I try my best to make sure Catalina's happy, and that makes me happy, too.
>
> As a future president, you should be concerned about what happens to your working-class people. We serve fast, convenient meals for families. We keep the city streets clean, and we help run airports. I fight for 15 and union rights, and I will continue to do so, to make sure every worker can live with dignity and respect.
>
> We've already won raises for 20 million workers. I hope that you will join us in protecting the American dream. No one in this country should have to work for less than $15 an hour or without the rights of being in a union. As our president, we need you to raise, pay, and tear down the barriers to opportunity. My daughter's life is counting on you.
>
> Sincerely, Andrea Meadors, Catalina's mom."

**Subject:** Single mother, McDonald's worker, Fight for $15 activist · **Cultural moment:** 2016 presidential election — Fight for $15 had won raises for 20 million workers; $10.50 Chicago minimum wage era

**Production role signals:**
- ✅ Field producing and subject sourcing (found and built trust with real minimum-wage worker)
- ✅ Documentary direction (first-person letter format — unusual, high-emotional resonance)
- ✅ Social optimization (epistolary format + political hook = high shareability)
- ✅ Advocacy journalism (piece functions as open letter to presidential candidates)

**Key details:** Subject: Andrea Meadors ("Andy"), McDonald's worker, Chicago · Daughter: Catalina, premature birth · Wage: $10.50/hour Chicago minimum · Qualifies for food stamps

**CV-ready bullets:**
- Produced first-person advocacy documentary at AJ+ profiling Fight for $15 activist Andy Meadors — McDonald's worker and single mother of premature infant; episodic letter-to-president format drove emotional resonance and shareability in 117-second social format

**AI-native portfolio caption:** First-person Fight for $15 documentary — McDonald's worker Andy Meadors writes to her future president in 1:57.

**Tags:** #DocumentaryJournalism #AdvocacyJournalism #FightFor15 #EconomicJustice #AJP #SocialFirst #WorkingClass #HumanInterest
